story:
The mansion was a beast—stone crouched on a hill, marble steps gleaming like teeth. Its windows stared bright and hard, daring anyone to call it less than perfect. Inside, perfection had a price. Morgan scrubbed on her knees in Mae’s primary bedroom, the rag burning her raw fingers. Mae—Jace’s sister—glided in with a glass of Chardonnay, dress unwrinkled, smile cool. Ken, her husband, filled the doorway like a guard. Jace, sweaty from hauling boxes, gave Morgan a tired, apologetic smile.

“Almost done?” he asked.

“Almost,” she said, swallowing what she wanted to say.

Mae set her glass down, lifted her phone, and trilled, “Time for the evaluation.” A flat, official voice answered. House Integrity. Monthly scoring. Reputation on the line. Mae panned the room on video: mirrors sharp, carpet lintless, a space that looked untouched by life.

“Perfect. Ten out of ten,” the voice declared.

Mae beamed. Morgan squeezed her rag until water bled out between her clenched fingers. The house didn’t shine because of Mae. It shone because of her.

Then the house groaned.

A scrape at the window. A dull thump. Ken scowled. “Raccoons.” Another scrape. Then a hand—pale, nails torn—slapped the glass. Another. And another. Figures staggered into view across the flawless lawn: costumes sagging and rotted as if a party had died and kept walking. A witch’s hat drooped over a ruined cheek. A clown mask hung crooked, smeared dark. A devil’s cape fluttered behind a body caged with ribs.

Glass shattered. The first one crashed into the room, jaw yanking wide, the smell sour and old. Mae screamed. “Fight it! Morgan, fight it!” Morgan grabbed a glittering shard from the floor and drove it into the thing’s throat. Foul blood sprayed. It gurgled and dropped. More bodies hit the frame, moans rolling like a tide.

“Run!” Jace shouted.

Ken swung a lamp; ceramic exploded; one monster fell. Another lunged. Teeth found flesh. Ken cried out and went down, swallowed by grasping hands, the carpet darkening beneath him.

“Ken!” Mae’s scream broke as Jace yanked her away.

They bolted, Morgan skidding on the slick floor as they tore down the hall. The portraits watched, silent and cold, as the living ran.

The Jeep waited outside. Morgan threw herself into the back and slammed the lock. Mae scrambled in through the passenger door and half fell into the front seat, sobbing. Jace grabbed the driver’s door—and hands clutched him. He hit the gravel, eyes wide, reaching toward the Jeep as the crowd closed over him. The keys, dropped in the scramble, were already dangling from the ignition.

“Jace!” Morgan pounded the glass until her fists hurt. For a heartbeat his gaze found hers. Then he was gone beneath them.

Something in Morgan snapped into focus. She hauled herself over the console, shoved Mae aside, jammed the key into the ignition, and the engine turned over. She stomped the gas. A corpse clung to the side of the Jeep, fingers hooked through the open passenger frame, jaws snapping; Mae kicked at its hands and face until it slid under the tires and disappeared. The mansion fell away in the mirror, shrinking into the dark as if it had never been real.

The Jeep hurtled forward, grief burning like a second heart in Morgan’s chest. Mae sobbed beside her, hands trembling, dress stained. The night carried distant, low moans, a sound you could feel more than hear. Behind them, the perfect house bled. Ahead, only road and the possibility of more monsters. Everyone else was gone. Only Morgan and Mae remained—shaken, stained, breathing.

Alive, for now.
